perturbation.original_text: NTC shall be deemed to have accepted a purchase order unless NTC provides written notice of rejection to ALFA AESAR within three (3) days of NTC's receipt of such order, not including weekends and nationally recognized holidays.
perturbation.changed_text: NTC shall be deemed to have accepted a purchase order unless NTC provides written notice of rejection to ALFA AESAR within one (1) day of NTC's receipt of such order, including weekends and holidays.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the rejection notification period to one day, including weekends and holidays, is likely unreasonable and potentially violates contract law principles requiring a reasonable opportunity to review and respond to purchase orders. The original clause excludes weekends and holidays, implying a consideration for adequate processing time.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-206. Offer and Acceptance in Formation of Contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC 2-206 dictates reasonable acceptance. Reducing response time to 1 day, including holidays, contradicts 'reasonable opportunity' to accept/reject, especially for complex orders, making acceptance potentially forced and violating good faith.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-206. Offer and Acceptance in Formation of Contract. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-206. Offer and Acceptance in Formation of Contract. (1) Unless otherwise unambiguously indicated by the language or circumstances (a) an offer to make a contract shall be construed as inviting acceptance in any manner and by any medium reasonable in the circumstances; (b) an order or other offer to buy goods for prompt or current shipment shall be construed as inviting acceptance either by a prompt promise to ship or by the prompt or current shipment of conforming or non-conforming goods, but such a shipment of non-conforming goods does not constitute an acceptance if the seller seasonably notifies the buyer that the shipment is offered only as an accommodation to the buyer. (2) Where the beginning of a requested performance is a reasonable mode of acceptance an offeror who is not notified of acceptance within a reasonable time may treat the offer as having lapsed before acceptance. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the time frame for rejecting a purchase order to one day, including weekends and holidays, from three days excluding weekends and holidays. This creates an inconsistency regarding a 'reasonable opportunity' to accept or reject, as per the Law Explanation. While Snippet 1 speaks to acceptance in general, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation highlight the concern that reducing the rejection period to one day may not provide a reasonable opportunity for NTC to review the purchase order, especially considering weekends and holidays, potentially forcing acceptance and contradicting good faith.

perturbation.original_text: 4.3 NTC shall invoice ALFA AESAR for payment of Products delivered to ALFA AESAR on a Net 30 day basis. Excepting any amounts disputed in good faith, balances unpaid after such period will be subject to a finance charge of 1% per month or may be offset against any balances owed by NTC to ALFA AESAR.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.3 NTC shall invoice ALFA AESAR for payment of Products delivered to ALFA AESAR on a Net 10 day basis. Excepting any amounts disputed in good faith, balances unpaid after such period will be subject to a finance charge of 5% per month or may be offset against any balances owed by NTC to ALFA AESAR.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the payment period to 10 days and increasing the finance charge to 5% per month could be considered usurious depending on the state and the specific transaction. The original net 30 terms and 1% finance charge are more standard business practices.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 892 - Making extortionate extensions of credit
perturbation.law_explanation: Charging 5% per month (60% annually) may constitute a criminally usurious rate, depending on state-specific usury laws and if it's considered an extortionate extension of credit. While the federal law primarily targets organized crime, extremely high interest rates can trigger scrutiny under state usury laws, making the clause unenforceable.

Making extortionate extensions of credit (a) Whoever makes any extortionate extension of credit, or conspires to do so, shall be fined under this title or imprisoned not more than 20 years, or both. (b) In any prosecution under this section, if it is shown that all of the following factors were present in connection with the extension of credit in question, there is prima facie evidence that the extension of credit
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a potentially usurious interest rate (5% per month, 60% annually) and a shortened payment period (10 days), which could violate state usury laws. The Law Explanation indicates that such a high interest rate may be considered criminally usurious. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 from 18 USC 892 focuses on extortionate extensions of credit, it demonstrates that excessively high interest rates can trigger legal scrutiny. The altered finance charge and payment terms create an inconsistency that could be deemed illegal under state usury laws, making the terms unenforceable.
